payload.text:
Esta es la parte central en medio de una llamada. Como veis, en la parte superior central se presentan los iconos de traducción y de transcripción para la conversación. Específicamente, los iconos de la izquierda son los de transcripción y los de la derecha son los de traducción.

Un poco más arriba en la foto se ven las líneas que se mueven durante la conversación; habría que juntarlas más para ganar espacio. También la calidad del WebRTC (la banda con dos manitas a los lados que se mueve horizontalmente) habría que mejorarla y ganar espacio en esa zona.

Todo esto sucede únicamente durante el estado de llamada.

**Refining UI Goals**

I'm currently analyzing the request to enhance the "active call" state within `SystemFeed.tsx`. My primary goal is a UI/UX improvement. This process will hone in on specifics to refine the initial target.


**Analyzing Call Interface Elements**

I'm now diving into the specifics. I'm focusing on the top central part of the call interface within `SystemFeed.tsx`. I've pinpointed the transcription and translation icons, and the WebRTC quality indicator. I'll need to figure out how to compact the audio visualization and make the entire interface aesthetically appealing, as the user requested.


**Implementing Visuals and Structure**

I'm now implementing the design from the user's screenshot into `SystemFeed.tsx`. My current focus is integrating an "Active Call Mode". This involves a state toggle to switch between the standard feed and the new active call view. I'm building out the new header with contact info and a timer. I'm also addressing the middle row: adding the language selector, toggles, and waveform visualization. Tightening the waveform and finding aesthetically pleasing display styles are key.


**Designing Visual Improvements**

I'm now focusing on specific visual improvements within `SystemFeed.tsx`. I'll style the WebRTC quality bar as a sleek gradient with connection indicators. I'm also implementing the right controls: a language selector and toggle buttons. My visual style will use the existing color palette with clean lines, rounded corners, and subtle shadows for a modern dashboard feel, and 'hollow' icons. Next, I'll update the top part of the component and integrate the call control header.


**Organizing Interface Elements**

I'm now focusing on arranging the transcription and translation controls for the active call view. I'll group them logically into left (local) and right (remote) sections. The left side will house the mic toggle, language dropdown, and transcription toggle. The right side will feature the speaker toggle, remote language selection, and translation toggle.

\n\n

I'm also drafting the code for key visual components. I'm imagining the waveform as a flex container with animating bars and the quality bar as a gradient with a marker. Icons will be sourced from `Lucide-react`. I'm working to reduce spacing between the waveform and controls for a more compact layout.


**Refining WebRTC Element**

I'm now iterating on the WebRTC quality bar; I intend to replace the thick bar with a sleek, interactive line. I plan to add a glowing dot, indicating latency, plus thumbs-up and thumbs-down icons for direct quality feedback. I'm focusing on making this an aesthetically pleasing element.


**Constructing Component Structure**

I'm now building out the React structure within `SystemFeed.tsx`. My focus is on the component's internal division, specifically the waveform, quality bar, and right controls. I'm focusing on their layout, making sure they are a flex container. My aim is to get a smooth, compact UI layout. Next I'll tackle the Tailwind styling, including making use of `Lucide-react`.


**Implementing Interactive Buttons**

I'm now focusing on user interaction and visual feedback for the buttons. My goal is to make these control elements intuitive, and responsive to user input. I'm focusing on the visual cues to be clear, and I'll focus on the Tailwind styling to make them appealing. I'll make sure they provide immediate confirmation of any user action.

Para mejorar la interfaz de la llamada activa, voy a rediseñar completamente la cabecera del componente `SystemFeed.tsx`.

Aquí tienes los cambios detallados que voy a aplicar basándome en tu descripción y la captura de pantalla:

1.  **Zona Central Compacta**: Voy a crear un panel de control unificado donde la visualización de audio (las líneas) y la barra de calidad (WebRTC) ocupen menos espacio vertical pero sean más informativas.
2.  **Barra WebRTC "Pro"**: Sustituiré la barra simple por una barra de gradiente con los iconos de "pulgar arriba/abajo" (ThumbsUp/ThumbsDown) que mencionas, con un indicador de latencia preciso.
3.  **Controles de Transcripción y Traducción**: Separaré claramente los controles:
    *   **Izquierda (Local/Transcripción):** Selector de idioma origen (bandera), micro y botón de transcripción.
    *   **Derecha (Remoto/Traducción):** Selector de idioma destino, altavoz y botón de traducción.
4.  **Estética**: Usaré un diseño más limpio, con fondos sutiles y bordes suaves para que parezca una herramienta profesional de telecomunicaciones.

Aquí tienes el código actualizado:

payload.text: La duración de la llamada realmente queda mal; yo creo que se puede mejorar. Las banderas yo las haría menos barrocas, quizás la banderita un poquito más simple, más minimalista.A la derecha, en la zona donde se hacen las llamadas telefónicas, está el botón de grabación. No sé si se podría mejorar el diseño, porque en este momento es uno de los elementos que más llama la atención en la pantalla al tener ese color rojo y el círculo rojo con el interior blanco.

**Assessing UI Improvement**

I'm currently focused on the user's request, specifically how to improve the UI for the active call and dialer interfaces. I'm starting by analyzing the user's feedback, which will help me identify the main goals of this project. Focusing on the call duration display, I can start to develop potential changes.


**Refining UI Elements**

I'm now diving into specific UI elements. The call duration display feels clunky, so I'm aiming for a digital timer aesthetic with a monospace font and subtle pulsing indicator. The language flags need a simpler, minimalist approach, and I am exploring icons instead of complex emojis. Finally, the recording button in the dialer is far too prominent. I need to make the recording button visually fit better with the rest of the UI but still make the user aware of its status (recording or not).


**Simplifying Language Flags**

I've been working on a new approach for the language flags. I've decided to move away from complex emojis, and I'm proposing simple SVG icons or text codes with a minimalist colored bar or dot. My refined approach is to use the 2-letter ISO code (e.g., "ES", "EN") styled cleanly within a badge, which should be the most minimalist option.

I'm also focusing on the record button in the dialer component, aiming for a cleaner visual style. I plan to use a toggle style: A clean button that turns red only when active.


**Implementing UI Changes**

I've been translating the design refinements into code. For the call duration, I'm swapping the orange text for a slate-colored pill with a monospace font and a pulsing dot. Regarding language flags, I'm replacing the emojis with cleaner text representations, such as "EN" or "ES" within small rounded boxes. Also, the recording button in the dialer is getting an overhaul; it's now an icon button that only turns red when active.


**Finalizing UI Implementation**

I'm now putting the final touches on the UI implementation, specifically making the Record button more subtle. When inactive, the record button will use `bg-white` and `text-slate-400`. When recording, the record button will be `bg-rose-50` with `text-rose-600`. The duration display will be changed to `00:04:21` in a tabular-nums font next to the phone number. Finally, I will use a small rounded rectangle `[ ES ]` with a chevron instead of a large emoji for the language flag.


**Validating Implemented Changes**

I've just finished a code review to confirm that all constraints and feedback have been addressed. The duration display, language flags, and record button styles are now all validated. I am currently focusing on fine-tuning the UI elements, like simplifying the language flag with a dropdown trigger and restyling the timer. I've corrected any errors I encountered during coding, and checked the file hierarchy.

Aquí tienes las mejoras solicitadas para pulir la interfaz, centrándonos en el minimalismo y en reducir la "agresividad" visual de ciertos elementos.

**Cambios realizados:**

1.  **Duración de la llamada:** He rediseñado el contador. Ahora utiliza una tipografía monoespaciada (tabular-nums) para evitar saltos, con un fondo gris muy suave y un diseño más técnico y limpio, eliminando el color naranja agresivo.
2.  **Banderas (Idiomas):** He eliminado los emojis. Ahora se utilizan códigos ISO (ES, EN) dentro de insignias minimalistas y elegantes. Es mucho menos "barroco" y más profesional.
3.  **Botón de Grabación (Dialer):** He cambiado completamente su estado de reposo. Ahora es un botón sutil (gris/blanco) que se integra con el resto de controles. Solo se vuelve rojo y parpadea cuando **está grabando activamente**.

Aquí tienes el código actualizado:

entries.description: Refined Active Call Header: Minimalist ISO language codes instead of emojis, and a cleaner, tabular-num timer design.

payload.text:
La duración de la llamada debería ser más grande y visualmente más atractiva. No me gusta la fuente que utilizas en algunas ocasiones, esa que parece de máquina de escribir; preferiría que no usaras ese tipo de letra.

Respecto al botón de grabar, creo que te has pasado. Está bien que lo hayas hecho más pequeño, pero ahora es tan minimalista que no tiene el mismo peso que el resto de los elementos. Debería quedar claro que es un botón de grabación; no hace falta que tenga tanto rojo como antes, pero sí debería conservar algo de color.

Por otro lado, veo que has eliminado las banderas directamente. Creo que podríamos mantenerlas, pero sin el diseño anterior. Mi sugerencia es:
- Incluir todas las banderas que puedas (Unión Europea, Estados Unidos, Inglaterra, Brasil, etc.).
- Utilizar un estilo muy minimalista.
- Eliminar las letras de las banderas, ya que sin ellas se refuerza la sensación de que estás en un sistema de traducción simultánea.

**Revising User Interface**

I'm zeroing in on the call duration display; the feedback indicates a strong dislike for the current timer. The typewriter font is the primary concern, making it visually unappealing, and the user feels the display is too small. Considering alternatives.


**Refining Visual Cues**

I'm now focusing on the visual weight of the dialer button. The user feedback is it needs more presence; it felt too minimal. Thinking of a record button aesthetic, retaining red but less intense than before. Also, I'm integrating the language flags. I'll include minimalist flags, removing text labels and adding flags for multiple countries to reflect the simultaneous translation.


**Designing Flag Indicators**

I'm now implementing the minimalist flag indicators. I've decided to create custom SVG paths for key flags like the EU, US, UK, and Brazil to achieve a clean, flat aesthetic, ensuring consistency and a professional look. I will need a `MinimalFlag` component for this. I've discarded the circular image approach. Using Lucide icons when possible.


**Implementing Record Button**

I'm making progress on the dialer's record button, working within `components/Dialer.tsx`. I'm moving away from an outline. I've decided on a soft red background, a bolder red border, and a distinct red circle icon for a more noticeable, but not overly aggressive, visual. I will need to refine this based on the existing design system. Now, time to move into code.

\n\n

I'm in the coding phase, updating `components/SystemFeed.tsx`. I've altered the timer's appearance to a larger size with a bolder, less monospaced style to improve visibility. I'm focusing on language flags, aiming for a visual representation without text. I'm creating a `Flag` component within the file and defining SVG flag styles for EU, US, UK, Brazil, and Spain, aiming for easily recognizable minimalist versions.


**Implementing Flag Selector**

I'm solidifying the flag selector logic. I'm hardcoding the visual setup to show the requested flags (Spain locally, and a toggle for UK/US/Brazil) without text labels. I'm focusing on the record button: It will be `w-12 h-12` with `bg-rose-50`, `border-2`, `border-rose-200`, `text-rose-600`, and hover states. I'll use a `div` element for the circle. I'm also refining the timer display, aiming for a modern, digital clock look with Open Sans/Inter numbers at `text-xl`, showing `00:04:23`. I will go back and check about keeping seconds.


**Constructing Minimal Flags**

I'm now writing inline SVGs, focusing on clean representations. I'm drafting: Spanish (red-yellow-red stripes), UK (blue background, white and red crosses), US (stripes, blue canton), Brazil (green, yellow rhombus, blue circle), and EU (blue circle with stars). I'm moving toward implementing these flags. Next, I plan to update `ActiveCallHeader` and language selector sections to utilize the new `MinimalFlag` component within `SystemFeed.tsx`.

I'm solidifying the flag selector logic. I'm hardcoding the visual setup to show the requested flags (Spain locally, and a toggle for UK/US/Brazil) without text labels. I'm focusing on the record button: It will be `w-12 h-12` with `bg-rose-50`, `border-2`, `border-rose-200`, `text-rose-600`, and hover states. I'll use a `div` element for the circle. I'm also refining the timer display, aiming for a modern, digital clock look with Open Sans/Inter numbers at `text-xl`, showing `00:04:23`. I will go back and check about keeping seconds.

Aquí tienes los cambios solicitados.

**Resumen de las mejoras:**

1.  **Duración de la llamada:** He eliminado la fuente "tipo máquina de escribir" (monoespaciada). Ahora utilizo la fuente principal (Sans) pero con un tamaño mucho más grande (`text-2xl`), negrita y `tabular-nums` para que los números no "bailen" pero se vean modernos y limpios.
2.  **Banderas Minimalistas:** He creado un componente de banderas SVG personalizadas y minimalistas. He eliminado totalmente las letras (ES, EN). Ahora se ven iconos gráficos nítidos de la Unión Europea, EE.UU., Reino Unido, Brasil y España, reforzando la sensación de herramienta de traducción visual.
3.  **Botón de Grabar:** He devuelto el "peso" al botón. Ya no es solo un borde fino. Ahora tiene un fondo rojo suave (`bg-rose-50`), un borde rojo definido y un círculo interior rojo sólido. Se distingue claramente del resto de controles grises sin ser tan agresivo como el bloque rojo sólido original.

Aquí está el código:

payload.text: ¿Puedes devolver a muchos botones las funcionalidades que tenían antes? He visto que han desaparecido funcionalidades en el histórico de llamadas y en el histórico de mensajes. Me da la sensación de que tenemos muchas menos funcionalidades de las que teníamos anteriormente.

**Pinpointing Lost Features**

I'm currently focused on dissecting the user's request. My primary goal is to clearly identify the lost functionality within the Activity Log – specifically, the missing buttons and features they've reported. I'm prioritizing understanding the exact nature of these elements and how they were used previously.


**Analyzing Missing Actions**

I've moved past merely identifying the lost features and am now deeply auditing `ActivityLog.tsx`. I'm examining each `ActivityItem` to find where the interactive buttons are missing, focusing on the current interactions, item displays, and any interaction failures. The detail view is a simple placeholder and needs full development. My next task will be mapping where potential clickable items should be, and confirming any pre-existing button features, and interaction states.


**Defining Feature Restoration**

I'm now outlining the specifics of what needs to be added back. The DetailView is currently a blank placeholder, which is not useful. The filtering is restricted to a simple icon, instead of a real menu. I'm focusing on defining and listing lost features: row actions (Call Back, Message, View Details, Play/Download Recording, Copy ID), filters (All, Calls, Messages, Missed, Voicemail), and the critical need for a functional DetailView to display and allow actions on transcripts/notes.



**Implementing Hover Actions**

I'm now focusing on interactive elements, specifically the hover actions. I plan to add a dynamic menu on row hover, with "Call," "Message," and "Play Recording" buttons. I'm also ensuring the existing "Flags" are visually interactive. Building a real `DetailView` is a priority, and it will feature a placeholder summary and transcription for the call, complete with a player UI, and action buttons. I will soon be sketching out code changes to the ActivityLog component.


**Structuring Detail View**

I'm now detailing the DetailView's structure. I will separate it into Header for contact information, a Toolbar for actions, and Content for transcripts and summaries. Additionally, I'll incorporate a dummy audio player visualization. I am also making sure that the new action buttons are appropriately positioned, and are z-indexed correctly so they are never hidden. I'm focusing on the row layout, and will add action buttons to the right, either replacing or overlaying the duration/direction information on hover. I will also be updating the `ActivityLog.tsx` component.

payload.text: PUEDES GENERAR DOS O TRES VERSIONES ADICIONALES CON DIFERENTES COLORES

**Considering App's Colors**

I'm currently focused on the implications of a request to design different color themes for an app. The initial analysis highlights the existing "Slate & Emerald" theme (gray/white/green). I'm considering the potential impact on user perception and the need for new themes beyond the initial one.


**Defining App Themes**

I've determined the initial themes to build out: Default, Midnight (dark mode), Professional (blue/grey), and Warm (sunset/orange). Now, I'm thinking I'll need a theme switcher to allow the user to toggle between these variations. This will be an essential element.


**Implementing Theme Context**

I'm now implementing the theme context! Instead of a full-scale Tailwind overhaul with CSS variables, my focus is on a `ThemeContext` that wraps the app and applies a theme class to the root. I will then utilize Tailwind's configuration and `group` class to handle visual changes. This approach strikes a balance between rapid iteration and minimizing code rework for "show me versions". I'll be focusing on the key layout components to incorporate the context.


**Refining Theme Strategy**

I'm now refining the theme strategy! I've decided to create a `ThemeContext` that exposes a `theme` string, reflecting the selected theme. A `useThemeClass(intent)` hook will provide the correct Tailwind classes, addressing the challenge of extensive file modifications. Previously, I was considering replacing the color names in each file. I'll implement a **Theme Picker** in the `Header`, adding a `data-theme` attribute to the body, which will drive color swapping using Tailwind configuration. I must work within the constraint of not altering `tailwind.config.js` directly.


**Developing Theme Selection**

I'm now zeroing in on an improved approach. I will create `ThemeContext.tsx` to wrap the app and read from this context. To minimize file changes, I'll concentrate on the accent and background colors. I'm considering using CSS variables via a style tag for `--primary`, `--bg-main`, and `--text-main`.  However, I've reframed to focus on "generating 2-3 versions" and will add a settings modal for theme toggling with these **3 Themes**: Trident Original, Ocean Blue, and a third.


**Implementing Dark Mode**

I'm now focused on implementing a dark/violet theme, dubbed "Midnight Pro." My immediate plan includes creating a `ThemeContext`, wrapping the app, and adding a theme selector in the header. I'm prioritizing updates to key layout components and using a `useTheme()` hook for dynamic color assignments. A helper method will handle color string interpolation.


**Prioritizing Light Variations**

My current plan prioritizes light mode color variations for simplicity and speed, fulfilling the request for "different colors" as branding. I'm focusing on three themes: Emerald (Original), Blue (Corporate), and Rose (Urgent/Warm). I'll update `App.tsx` for background changes based on the theme, and replace hardcoded colors in `components/*.tsx` with dynamic values from the hook. I'll defer a robust dark mode to ensure quality and meet delivery timelines.


**Implementing Theme Context**

I'm now drafting the context and hook to select and apply colors. I've defined `themes` with primary and secondary color pairings. My hook now allows access to theme colors within components, and I'll focus on replacing hardcoded values with dynamic variables from the context. I'll need to update multiple components to be dynamic. I am executing the required changes now, step by step.

```typescript
export const themes = {
  emerald: { primary: 'emerald', secondary: 'slate' },
  blue: { primary: 'blue', secondary: 'gray' },
  rose: { primary: 'rose', secondary: 'stone' },
}
```

```typescript
const { theme } = useTheme();
// ...
className={`bg-${theme.primary}-500 ...`}
```

```typescript
// types.ts
export type ThemeOption = keyof typeof themes;
```


**Mapping Theme Colors**

I'm now focusing on ensuring reliable color application across components. I've realized the need for an explicit mapping object for theme colors due to potential JIT compiler limitations in the browser environment. I am planning to replace the dynamic template strings with a predefined mapping for `emerald`, `blue`, and `rose`, ensuring that the correct colors will be applied for each theme.


**Adjusting Semantic Colors**

I'm now grappling with semantic color application. While I'll use template strings for brevity, I've acknowledged reliance on CDN parsing. I'm focusing on "semantic" colors: green for original, blue for corporate, and red for high contrast/warm. Specifically, I'm analyzing the existing component colors; for instance, the Dialer uses "emerald-500", but `SystemFeed.tsx` utilizes `rose` to denote "recording". My plan will be to leave semantic colors as is and swap the brand color. I will be updating Header.tsx to include the theme switcher, updating the theme context, and integrating it to Index.tsx.


**Updating Component Themes**

I'm now making steady progress on integrating the selected themes. I'm focusing on updating `App`, `ActivityLog`, `SystemFeed`, `Dialer`, and `TeamPanel` to consume the new `ThemeContext`. I've outlined the files to update, and must ensure perfect XML output, given this is an app. I'm prioritizing Green, Blue, and Violet/Purple themes, with Emerald, Blue, and Violet (respectively) as the color options. I'm drafting the `ThemeProvider` and `useTheme` hook, with a plan for component updates. I need to be careful with semantic colors during find-and-replace, e.g. "recording", so will ignore these.


**Refining Branding Colors**

I'm now prioritizing semantic color consistency as I build out each theme, and will be introducing a `primaryColor` variable within the components. My analysis revealed a significant reliance on 'emerald' for *both* branding and semantic elements in the base theme. This presents a challenge for theme switching! My approach will be to maintain semantic meaning (e.g., green for "connected") while selectively updating branding colors. For the "Blue" theme, tabs, borders, icons, and highlights will use blue, but the "call" button will remain green.

2.  **Streaming en Tiempo Real**: Utiliza `generateContentStream` para que el texto aparezca instantáneamente mientras se genera.
3.  **Integración en Dashboard**: He añadido una tarjeta destacada "Flash Lite Chat" en el "Command Center" (Dashboard) con el icono de un rayo.
4.  **UI Adaptativa**: El chat hereda los estilos *Glassmorphism* y los colores corporativos (Emerald/Slate) del resto de la aplicación, funcionando perfectamente en modo claro y oscuro.

payload.text: Las barritas de sonido, cómo se pueden implementar para que suban y bajen.

**Initiating the Analysis**

I'm initially focused on breaking down the animation request. My primary goal is to simulate realistic audio activity in the visualizer bars ("barritas de sonido"). I'm currently assessing the user's provided context and its implications for the animation design.


**Evaluating Animation Options**

I'm now weighing the implementation routes for dynamic visuals in the sound bars. The existing `SystemFeed.tsx` file offers a static foundation with `animate-pulse` CSS animations. I am considering two approaches: Real-time audio analysis with the Web Audio API or a simpler simulated animation approach using CSS and/or JavaScript. The pros and cons of each are actively being evaluated to determine the best fit.


**Prioritizing Simulated Realism**

I've determined a simulated animation is optimal for this mock app. I'll need to create dynamic heights for the visualizer bars. I'm focusing on the implementation using React state, `requestAnimationFrame`, and some CSS transitions for a techy feel. I'm aiming for an array of bars with fluctuating heights that mimic speech patterns. It's a hybrid approach.


**Refining CSS and State**

I'm now zeroing in on a CSS keyframes approach with randomized delays and durations. However, I've corrected my initial assumption regarding the existing implementation, which already uses `animate-pulse` and random inline styles. I realize my focus should be implementing the "up and down" motion the user requests. I'm moving toward a React component that manages heights with `requestAnimationFrame` for smooth updates, replacing the existing static state within the render output. This approach should yield a better visual.


**Optimizing for Performance**

I'm now streamlining the implementation. Initially, I thought about using `requestAnimationFrame`, but I've realized updating heights 60 times a second might be overkill. I'm leaning toward a CSS Module/Tailwind + Inline Style solution with a simplified interval, as it's lighter for a UI demo and easier to manage active/inactive states. I'm exploring an approach that leverages a specific set of keyframes for Y-axis scaling.


**Implementing Height Animation**

I've homed in on a performant "fake" visualizer technique. It's essentially CSS-driven: random durations, custom height keyframes (`0% { height: 20% } 50% { height: 80% } 100% { height: 20% }`), and `animation-delay` offsets. Examining the code in `SystemFeed.tsx`, I've identified that `Math.random()` creates static heights instead of continuous animation. I'll need a custom keyframe in global styles to change the height animation or use a React component to randomize the height periodically.


**Refining Waveform Implementation**

I'm now zeroing in on a React-Interval approach for the visualizer. I'm building a `Waveform` component within `SystemFeed.tsx`. I will leverage `useEffect` with `setInterval` to generate randomized bar heights, ensuring smooth transitions with CSS. I am targeting an update frequency of 10 times per second, and will address a starting point for bars via state initialization. I am also working on adding a height transition so that the bars slide rather than jump during state updates.


**Enhancing Visual Realism**

I'm now focusing on visual refinements, aiming for a more polished look. I will ensure silence bars remain small, while active bars exhibit emerald coloration. I am implementing the user's "suban y bajen" request by generating random heights for the bars with transitions. I will also replace the static visualizer with the new dynamic logic. I will integrate this revised visualizer into `ActiveCallHeader`.

Para que las barras de sonido cobren vida y se muevan de forma fluida (simulando una voz hablando), necesitamos cambiar la implementación estática por una dinámica.

La mejor manera de hacerlo sin complicar el código con APIs de audio reales es utilizando un **intervalo que actualice las alturas aleatoriamente** combinado con una **transición CSS** para suavizar el movimiento.

Aquí tienes la actualización para `components/SystemFeed.tsx`.

### Cambios realizados:
1.  **Animación Fluida**: He sustituido el array estático por un estado `bars` que se actualiza cada 100ms.
2.  **Suavizado Visual**: He añadido `transition-all duration-100 ease-in-out` a cada barra. Esto hace que en lugar de "saltar" de tamaño, la barra se deslice hacia arriba y abajo, creando un efecto orgánico muy agradable.
3.  **Simulación de Voz**: La lógica genera valores aleatorios (entre 20% y 100%) para simular actividad constante.

entries.description: Implement dynamic audio waveform animation using setInterval and CSS transitions to simulate active speech.
